People who claim to be followers of God and seem to be suffering greatly are likely to complain that God is being harsh with them. Here is an answer for those complaints: God never takes away anyone’s blessings except as a consequence of their sins. The Jews were sent into Babylon because of their idolatry, a sin that broke the covenant; and they were ultimately rejected for crucifying the Lord of glory. God called on them to abandon their sins and prevent their own destruction. Finally, the Son came to his own people, but they did not receive him. When God calls people to happiness, and they refuse to respond, they are justly left to be miserable. To quiet any doubts about his power, evidence of it is given. The miracles that accompanied his suffering and death proclaimed that he was the Son of God.

Because Jesus was both God and man in one person, we find him sometimes speaking, or being spoken of, as Lord God; at other times, as a man and the servant of Jehovah. He came to declare truths that comfort those with broken, contrite hearts, those who are weary of sin and troubled by afflictions. And just as the Holy Spirit was upon him so he could speak in ways no human had spoken before; so the same divine influence daily moved him to pray, preach the gospel, and receive and deliver the whole will of the Father. The Father justified the Son when he accepted his satisfaction for the sin of humanity. Christ speaks on behalf of all believers. Who would dare be an enemy to those to whom he is a Friend? Or who will contend with those whom he is an Advocate? Thus St. Paul applies it.

A child of God fears incurring his displeasure. This grace usually appears most in believers when they are struggling, when other graces seem absent. Those who truly fear God obey the voice of Christ. A sincere servant of God may for a long time lack awareness of eternal happiness. What is likely to be an effective remedy in this sad situation? Let him trust in the name of the Lord; and let him rely on the promises of the covenant, and build his hopes on them. Let him trust in Christ, trust in that name of his, The Lord our Righteousness; rely on God as his God, through a Mediator. Sinners who presume are warned not to trust in themselves. Their own merit and sufficiency are worthless to them. Temporary comforts are like sparks, short-lived and quickly gone; yet people of this world seek to warm themselves with them while they last, and walk with pride and pleasure in their light. Those who make the world their comfort and their own righteousness their confidence will certainly meet bitterness at the end. A godly person’s way may be dark, but his end will be peace and everlasting light. A wicked person’s way may be pleasant, but his final state forever will be complete darkness.
